coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots-functionalized C18 hydrophobic/hydrophilic stationary phase for high performance liquid chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Qi
Chen, Lixiao
Gao, Jie
Dong, Shuqing
Li, Hui
Di, Duolong
Zhao, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were chosen as functional material to improve the separation performance of C18 column since GQDs could provide multiple interactions such as hydrophilic, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions. In this study, a novel octadecyl modified GQDs-bonded silica (C18/GQDs/SiO2) stationary phase was prepared and applied in reversed-phase and hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography. This column showed satisfactory separation performance for both hydrophobic, polar and hydrophilic compounds including polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, alkylbenzenes, anilines, phenols, aromatic acids, alkaloids, nucleosides and nucleobases. Through investigating the impact of organic solvent content on retention, it was found this new stationary phase had typical characteristics of hydrophobic/hydrophilic chromatography. Compared with commercial C18 column, this column showed better separation performance for polar aromatic compounds because the introduction of GQDs provided more interactions such as π-π stacking, hydrophilic and hydrogen bonding interaction with analytes. To get an in-depth understanding of the retention mechanism, linear solvation energy relationship model was established for both C18/GQDs/SiO2 and C18 columns, theoretically calculated data indicated that C18/GQDs/SiO2 column had higher π-π stacking and hydrogen-bonding acceptance ability. C18/GQDs composite stationary phase equipped with hydrophobic/hydrophilic properties has great prospect in separation science.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
C18
Reversed-phase liquid chromatography
Hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography
Linear solvation energy relationship

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Separation of Engelhardt standard test mixtures

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405162 , 21405161 , 21675163 and 21702210 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic process of C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase (the schematic diagram is for illustration purposes only and does not suggest the actual number and position of the C18 ligands).
FTIR spectra of NH2/SiO2, GQDs/SiO2 and C18/GQDs/SiO2.
The LSCM images of (a) NH2/SiO2, (b) GQDs/SiO2 and (c) C18/GQDs/SiO2 under 405 nm excitation.

Chromatograms for the separation of (a) PAHs, (b) alkylbenzenes on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column. Analytes: (a) (1) benzene, (2) naphthalene, (3) anthracene, (4) acenaphthene, (5) fluorene, (6) phenanthrene; (b) (1) benzene, (2) methylbenzene, (3) ethylbenzene, (4) n-propylbenzene, (5) isobutylbenzene, (6) phenylpentane; Mobile phase: (a) 55% MeOH/H2O (v/v), (b) 60% MeOH/H2O (v/v); flow rate: 0.8 mL/min.
Plot of log k versus the number of methylene at different mobile phase compositions. MeOH/H2O system was used as mobile phase and the percentage of MeOH was labeled.

Chromatograms for the separation of phenols on (a) C18/GQDs/SiO2 column and (b) commercial C18 column. Peak identity: (1) m-aminophenol, (2) resorcinol, (3) phenol, (4) 2,5-dimethylphenol, (5) 2-naphthol, (6) p-tert-butylphenol. Mobile phase: (a) 30% ACN/H2O (v/v), (b) 40% ACN/H2O (v/v); flow rate, 1.0 mL/min.

Chromatograms for the separation of mixture on (a) C18/GQDs/SiO2 column and (b) commercial C18 column. Peak identity: (1) aniline, (2) theophylline, (3) phloroglucinol, (4) phenol, (5) 4-nitroaniline, (6) camptothecin, (7) 2,5-dimethylphenol, (8) benzoic acid, (9) phthalic acid, (10) acetylsalicylic acid, (11) p-tert-butylphenol, (12) 4-methylcinnamic acid, (13) diphenylamine. Mobile phase: (a) 30% ACN/0.1% formic acid water solution (v/v), (b) 40% ACN/0.1% formic acid water solution (v/v); flow rate, 1.0 mL/min.
Plot of log k versus volume fraction of ACN in mobile phase.

Chromatograms for the separation of nucleoside bases on (a) C18/GQDs/SiO2 column and (b) commercial C18 column. Peak identity: (1) thymine, (2) uracil, (3) adenine, (4) cytosine, (5) guanine. Mobile phase: (a) 90% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate (v/v), (b) 5% MeOH/H2O (v/v); flow rate, 1.0 mL/min.

Chromatograms for the separation of nucleosides on (a) C18/GQDs/SiO2 column and (b) commercial C18 column. Peak identity: (1) thymidine, (2) uridine, (3) adenosine, (4) cytidine, (5) guanosine. Mobile phase: (a) 90% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate (v/v), (b) 5% MeOH/H2O (v/v); flow rate, 1.0 mL/min.

Chromatograms for the separation of alkaloids on (a) C18/GQDs/SiO2 column and (b) commercial C18 column. Peak identity: (1) evodiamine, (2) caffeine, (3) theobromine, (4) theophylline, (5) jateorhizine, (6) fangchinoline. Mobile phase: (a) 90% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate (v/v), (b) 35% ACN/20 mM ammonium acetate (v/v); flow rate, 1.0 mL/min.
Experimental log k versus calculated log k plot for C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase.
Elemental analysis and specific surface area data of NH2/SiO2, GQDs/SiO2 and C18/GQDs/SiO2 composites.
System coefficients and statistics of C18/GQDs/SiO2 and C18 columns.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots-functionalized C18 hydrophobic/hydrophilic stationary phase for high performance liquid chromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were chosen as functional material to improve the separation performance of C18 column since GQDs could provide multiple interactions such as hydrophilic, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions. In this study, a novel octadecyl modified GQDs-bonded silica (C18/GQDs/SiO2) stationary phase was prepared and applied in reversed-phase and hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography. This column showed satisfactory separation performance for both hydrophobic, polar and hydrophilic compounds including polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, alkylbenzenes, anilines, phenols, aromatic acids, alkaloids, nucleosides and nucleobases. Through investigating the impact of organic solvent content on retention, it was found this new stationary phase had typical characteristics of hydrophobic/hydrophilic chromatography. Compared with commercial C18 column, this column showed better separation performance for polar aromatic compounds because the introduction of GQDs provided more interactions such as π-π stacking, hydrophilic and hydrogen bonding interaction with analytes. To get an in-depth understanding of the retention mechanism, linear solvation energy relationship model was established for both C18/GQDs/SiO2 and C18 columns, theoretically calculated data indicated that C18/GQDs/SiO2 column had higher π-π stacking and hydrogen-bonding acceptance ability. C18/GQDs composite stationary phase equipped with hydrophobic/hydrophilic properties has great prospect in separation science.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) are considered as good separation materials in chromatography [1–9]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new type of CNMs, have some unique characteristics such as small dimension, high thermostability and good water solubility, which make them easier to be immobilized on the silica support. GQDs not only possess highly delocalized conjugate system of π-electron but also have abundant oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxy and carboxyl, which give them multifunctional properties including hydrophobic, hydrophilic, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding properties. In recent years, GQDs are gradually applied in GC [10], CEC [11] and HPLC [12–15] showing satisfactory chromatographic separation performance. According to our previous research [12], GQDs-bonded silica stationary phase showed mixed retention mechanism including hydrophobic, π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding and hydrophilic interactions. GQDs-based stationary phases have great prospect in the development of hydrophobic/hydrophilic stationary phases. Hydrophobic/hydrophilic stationary phases have obtained great attention in the field of chromatography since they have strong separation performance for all kinds of compounds and thus good applicability in the analysis of complex samples especially in the case of liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [16].

C18 column which depends on hydrophobic interaction to realize separation, is the most commonly used reversed-phase column in HPLC since its excellent separation performance for many kinds of compounds. Despite all this, the pure octadecyl stationary phases still have some disadvantages such as low compatibility with highly aqueous eluent and insufficient selectivity towards polar and highly hydrophilic solutes, which results in their limited applicability in the separation of biological and botanical samples. With the in-depth study of natural products, biological medicine, proteomic and metabolomics, separation of polar and highly hydrophilic compounds is of great significance. To improve the separation performance of pure C18 stationary phases, polar groups such as diol and ionic liquids were used to combine with octadecyl forming a series of polar-embedded [17–25], polar-endcapped [18] and polar-copolymerized [26–29] C18 stationary phases. The presence of a polar segment in the stationary phases introduced polar interaction with solutes and thus broader analyte coverage. Developing polar-C18 stationary phases equipped with both hydrophobic and hydrophilic properties is of great significance in separation science.

Considering the multiple properties of GQDs, we think GQDs are suitable functional materials to be combined with octadecyl forming usable polar-C18 stationary phases with hydrophobic/hydrophilic properties. At present, graphene oxide and carbon nanotubes have been combined with octadecyl to form novel C18/CNMs composite stationary phases. According to Liang's [30] and Mayadunne's [29] report, the introduction of graphene oxide or carbon nanotubes in the stationary phases introduced π-π interaction and thus modulated the retention of analytes. Unfortunately, the polar and hydrophilic properties of these CNMs were not mentioned. To the best of our knowledge, preparation and application of C18/GQDs composite stationary phase has not been reported. Choosing GQDs as polar segment, multiple polar interactions could be introduced and enhanced separation performance may be realized.

Hence, a novel C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase was prepared by covalent bonding method and used in reversed-phase (RP) and hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC). Alkylbenzenes and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) were chosen as probes to evaluate the hydrophobic selectivity and aromatic selectivity of this new stationary phase. Polar compounds such as anilines, phenols, aromatic acids and hydrophilic compounds such as alkaloids, nucleosides and bases were chosen as probes to evaluate the polar and hydrophilic selectivity of this new stationary phase. A commercial C18 column was used as a reference to investigate the retention interactions introduced by GQDs and the impact of GQDs on separation performance. To get an in-depth understanding of the retention mechanism, linear solvation energy relationship (LSER) model was established for both the new column and commercial C18 column to obtain magnitude estimate of individual interaction.

Silica gel (300 m2/g, 100 Å, 5 µm in diameter) was supplied by Fuji Silysia Chemical Ltd (Japan). 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), octadecylamine and reagents (HPLC grade) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Meryer Chemical Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All chemicals used for chromatographic separation were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China).

Elemental analysis was performed on vario EL (Elementar, Germany). The fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra was acquired on an IFS120HR Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) images were obtained on a FLUOVIEW FV1200 biological confocal laser scanning microscope (OLYMPUS, Japan). Results for energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) were obtained using a JSM-5600LV Scanning Electron Microscope Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (JEOL, Japan). The data of specific surface area were obtained on the nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms using an ASAP2010 surface analysis instrument (Micromeritics, USA). Thermogravimetric analysis was performed on a 449F3 simultaneous thermal analyzer (Netzsch, Germany).

GQDs were prepared from GO aqueous dispersion using a hydrothermal synthesis method with H2O2 as an etching agent and ammonia as an assistant according to previously reported methods [31]. The particle size of the prepared GQDs can be identified as around 20 nm and the main oxygen-containing functional groups of the prepared GQDs were hydroxy, carboxyl and epoxy/ether according to our previously characterization [12,13].

The schematic diagram of the synthetic process was shown in Fig. 1. GQDs bonded silica composite was obtained by covalently coupling the carboxyl groups of GQDs and the amino groups of aminosilica as previously reported [12,13]. According to our previous experiments, the prepared GQDs have hydroxy, carboxyl and epoxy/ether [12,13]. However, the exact structure of GQDs was not sure at present, thus the number and position of carboxyl groups in GQDs were not sure. The schematic diagram is for illustration purposes only and does not suggest the actual number and position of the C18 ligands. The specific experimental process is as follows. First, 0.29 g EDC (1.5 mmol) and 0.17 g NHS (1.5 mmol) were added into 300 mL GQDs aqueous dispersion (1 mg/mL) and the mixture was stirred for 30 min to activate the carboxyl groups of GQDs. Then, 2.5 g aminosilica was added to the mixture and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. After the reaction is completed, GQDs/SiO2 were washed with water and methanol by filtration for several times and then were dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. Second, 2.5 g GQDs/SiO2 composite was dispersed in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and 0.29 g EDC (1.5 mmol) and 0.17 g NHS (1.5 mmol) were added into above dispersion to activate the residual carboxyl groups of GQDs/SiO2. After half an hour, 1.0 g of octadecylamine were added into above dispersion and the dispersion was stirred for 12 h at 45 ℃. Finally, the products were washed with DMF, chloroform and methanol for several times and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h.

The C18/GQDs/SiO2 composite (about 2.0 g) were packed into stainless steel tubes (150 mm × 4.6 mm i.d.) by a slurry packing method under a constant packing pressure of 40 MPa using 1,4-dioxane and carbon tetrachloride (1/1, v/v) as homogenate solvents and methanol as propulsion solvent.

All chromatographic separation was performed on a Waters HPLC system (Waters, USA), consisted of a Waters 515 HPLC pump, a Rheodyne 7725i injector equipped with 20 μL sample loop and a Waters 2487 double λ absorbance UV detector. The chromatographic separation performance of C18/GQDs/SiO2 column was evaluated in comparison with commercial C18 column (WondaSil C18 Superb 5 µm, 150 mm × 4.6 mm i.d.). The detection wavelength was at 254 nm.

Fig. 2 showed the FTIR spectra of NH2/SiO2, GQDs/SiO2 and C18/GQDs/SiO2. Compared to NH2/SiO2, the enhanced peak at 1718 cm−1 (C˭O in COOH) of GQDs/SiO2 indicated the covalent attachment of GQDs to aminosilica. The enhanced peaks at 2959/2928 cm−1 which were assigned to the stretching vibration of CH2 indicated the successful preparation of C18/GQDs/SiO2. The peaks at 1650 cm−1 which appeared in NH2/SiO2, GQDs/SiO2 and C18/GQDs/SiO2 may belong to strong scissors band of water absorption. From the LSCM images ( Fig. 3), homogeneous fluorescence was observed on the surface of GQDs/SiO2 and C18/GQDs/SiO2, which indicated uniform bonding reaction of GQDs with aminosilica. Elemental analysis and specific surface area (A s) data of NH2/SiO2, GQDs/SiO2 and C18/GQDs/SiO2 were listed in Table 1. The gradually increased carbon and nitrogen content indicated the successful preparation of C18/GQDs/SiO2 composite. From the nitrogen content increasing from 1.44% to 2.17%, the content of octadecylamine bonded to the surface of GQDs/SiO2 was calculated to be 52.14 mmol g−1. The calculation formula of the content of octadecylamine is as follow: content of octadecylamine (mmol g−1) = N% × 1000/M. N% represents the percentage of the increased nitrogen content and M is the atomic mass of nitrogen [32]. The A s of GQDs/SiO2 increased slightly compared to NH2/SiO2, this might be due to the large A s of GQDs. However, the A s decreased after bonding of C18, this might be due to that C18 occupied some active sites of GQDs, and this phenomenon could also be seen in reference [30]. EDS data (Fig. S1) showed the predominant elements in the prepared C18/GQDs/SiO2 are C, O, Si and N. Furthermore, the organic content of the prepared C18/GQDs/SiO2 was evaluated by thermogravimetric analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. S2. The first weight loss was observed at 20–50 °C, which was attributed to the evaporation of residual solvent. Over 150 °C, the organic component of C18/GQDs/SiO2 started to degrade. The organic weight fraction of this C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase was about 16% according to mass loss.

Fig. 4 showed the chromatograms of PAHs and alkylbenzenes separated on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column. All the tested analytes were efficiently separated on this new column, which indicated the good hydrophobic and aromatic selectivity of this column. It has often been observed in RP-LC that retention factor (k) values for the members of a homologous series increase exponentially with the number of homologous units [33]. Plots of the log k versus the number of methylene groups of alkylbenzenes are shown in Fig. 5. It was found log k values versus the number of methylene groups resulted in a straight line with good correlation at different mobile phase compositions. The experimental results indicated that this C18/GQDs/SiO2 column had good hydrophobic selectivity. Compared with the separation performance of GQDs/SiO2 [12], this column shows obvious enhancements in hydrophobic performance. This is due to the grafting of C18 ligands. C18 ligands play an important role in the hydrophobic performance of this stationary phase. We could expect that the chromatographic property of the stationary phase could be affected by C18 ligand numbers and immobilization sites. The heterogeneity of C18 ligand number and position would influence the hydrophobic selectivity of the stationary phase and thus the separation performance in RP-LC.

Upon increasing the proportion of organic modifier in a RP system, k values of non-polar samples are often found to decrease exponentially [34]. This corresponds to a linear relationship between log k and the volume or weight fraction of organic solvent.
(1)
l o g
k = l o g
k
w
–
S φ
where k w is the extrapolated k value in pure water, φ is the volume fraction of organic solvent, S is the slope of log k versus φ when fitted to a linear regression model.

Fig. S3 displayed the dependence of log k on the volume fraction of MeOH, which fitted well to the Eq. (1) at a range of 50–80% MeOH content with correlation coefficients of 0.990–0.997. With the increase of MeOH content in mobile phase, the retention for all the tested solutes decreased continuously. The experimental results indicated that this C18/GQDs/SiO2 column had typical characteristic of RP-LC.

Anilines, phenols and aromatic acids were chosen as test probes to investigate the chromatographic performance of this C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase for polar compounds in RP-LC. Commercial C18 column was used as a reference to investigate the impact of GQDs on separation. Fig. 6 showed the chromatograms of six phenols separated on C18/GQDs/SiO2 and commercial C18 columns. The tested phenols were better separated on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column within shorter analysis time in comparison with a C18 column. The better selectivity of C18/GQDs/SiO2 column is the result of its mixed retention mechanism. The separation ability of C18 column depends on its hydrophobic property. The interposition of GQDs in the C18 stationary phase introduced other retention interactions such as π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions. The better selectivity for m-aminophenol and resorcinol of C18/GQDs/SiO2 column could be due to the hydrogen bonding interaction between GQDs and analytes. 2,5-dimethylphenol and 2-naphthol obtained higher resolution on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column because they formed different π-π interaction with GQDs. With the help of hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking interactions, analytes with similar hydrophobicity achieved better separation on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column. Anilines could also be separated efficiently on this column (Fig. S4). 3-bromoaniline and 1-aminonaphthalene obtained better resolution on this column than on C18 column, which further indicated the positive effect of π-π stacking interaction introduced by GQDs. Fig. S5 showed the chromatograms of six aromatic acids separated on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column and commercial C18 column. The tested aromatic acids were all separated completely on these two columns under identical chromatographic conditions. From the contrasting chromatograms we found that phthalic acid had different retention behaviour on the two columns. Phthalic acid whose polarity was stronger than benzoic acid showed stronger retention than benzoic acid on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column, which further reflected the presence of polar interaction on this new column. Despite all this, the overall retention order of the tested solutes on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column was in line with that on C18 column, which indicated that hydrophobic interaction played a leading role during the separation process.

Fig. 7 showed the chromatograms of mixture of anilines, aromatic acids, alkaloids and phenols separated on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column and commercial C18 column. The retention time of compound 13 was longer than 30 min on C18 column, so its chromatographic peak was not appeared in Fig. 7(b). For the polar mixture, C18/GQDs/SiO2 column showed better separation performance than C18 column in the same analysis time because the presence of GQDs in the stationary phase provided polar interactions with analytes. We thought the good separation performance of C18/GQDs/SiO2 column came from its mixed retention mechanism including hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking and hydrophobic interaction, and hydrophobic interaction played a leading role since the existence of octadecyl.

According to our previous work, GQDs bonded silica stationary phase possessed the performance of HILIC [12]. To investigate the chromatographic performance of this C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase, nucleosides were chosen as probes to explore the effect of ACN content on retention. Plot of log k versus volume fraction of ACN in mobile phase were shown in Fig. 8. It was found that the retention of nucleosides decreased with the ACN content from 20% to 40%, and then increased with the ACN content increasing from 40% to 90%, which meant C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase switched from RP-LC to HILIC. When ACN content exceeded 60% in mobile phase, the stationary phase was occupied by a water-rich layer thus hydrophilic interaction emerged. This C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase had the typical characteristics of hydrophobic/hydrophilic chromatography, which enabled it to separate all kinds of compounds.

Fig. 9 and Fig. 10 showed the chromatograms of nucleosides and bases separated on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column and commercial C18 column. From the contrasting chromatograms we saw the five nucleosides and corresponding bases were better separated on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column. These hydrophilic compounds had very weak retention on C18 column, so they could not get good resolution. However, the introduction of GQDs in stationary phase introduced hydrophilic interaction, which enabled strong polar compounds to obtain good separation under hydrophilic chromatographic conditions. Fig. 11 showed the chromatograms of alkaloids separated on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column and commercial C18 column. From the separation results we saw these alkaloids could be better separated on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column within the same analysis time. Fangchinoline had strong retention on C18 column, so its chromatographic peak was not appeared in 30 min. The retention orders of the tested compounds on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column and C18 column were not reversed completely, though the test conditions were under hydrophilic and hydrophobic chromatographic conditions, respectively. This may be due to the reason that the prepared C18/GQDs/SiO2 column had mixed retention mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of Engelhardt standard test mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: Traditional approaches for characterization of column properties are based on the retention of selected test compounds at specified mobile phase composition. Engelhardt et al. introduced thiourea, aniline, phenol, p-toluidine, toluene and ethylbenzene as test analytes for hydrophobicity and silanol activity [35]. The evaluation should be performed in the "wetted" state, i.e. where the interaction of the bonded alkyl groups with the eluent components is stronger than with themselves. This is true as long as the water content does not exceed 50% (v/v) [35]. Methanol-water [55:45 (v/v)] was found to be optimum for both RP-C8 and RP-C18 column evaluation [36]. Hydrophobicity was measured by the relative retention of toluene and ethylbenzene, and silanol activity was measured by the relative retention of aniline and phenol. Generally, the elution of aniline before phenol indicate low silanol activity and suitability of the column for separation of basic compounds. Fig. S6 showed the separation result of Engelhardt standard test mixtures on C18/GQDs/SiO2 column. The selectivity factor (α) of toluene and ethylbenzene was 1.60, which indicated the good hydrophobic selectivity. Aniline was eluted before phenol and αphenol/aniline was 2.21, which demonstrated low silanol activity of this column. This experimental result indicated that most silanol were sheltered by GQDs.

Both hydrophobic, polar and hydrophilic compounds were efficiently separated on this stationary phase. This novel stationary phase was equipped with the good separation performance of C18 for hydrophobic compounds and the separation ability of GQDs for polar and hydrophilic compounds, which made it show satisfactory separation performance for several kinds of compounds under RP-LC and HILIC.

To get an in-depth understanding of the retention mechanism, LSER model was established to obtain magnitude estimate of individual interaction by following equation which was proposed by Chirita et al. [37] according to original Abraham equation.
(2)
l o g
k = c + e E + s S + a A + b B + v V +
d
-
D
-
+
d
+
D
+

In this equation, capital letters represent the solute descriptors, while lower case letters represent the system constants, related to the complementary effect of the stationary phase on these interactions. k is solute retention factor. c is the model intercept term, is not assigned any chemical significance. E is the excess molar refraction and models polarizability contributions from n and π electrons. S is the solute dipolarity/polarizability. A and B are the solute overall hydrogen-bond acidity and basicity. V is the McGowan characteristic volume. D− represents the negative charge carried by anionic and zwitterionic species, and D+ represents the positive charge carried by cationic and zwitterionic species.

For the LSER analysis, 38 solutes consisted of neutral, acidic, basic, and amphoteric molecules (Table S1) were analyzed under RP mobile phase conditions: ACN/100 mM ammonium acetate aqueous solution (
pH
w
w
4.0), 40:60 (v/v). The apparent mobile phase
pH
w
s
was measured with a glass electrode calibrated with aqueous calibration buffers and was 4.8. Charge related descriptors D+ and D− at
pH
w
w
4.0 were calculated according to equations in reference [37]. Other solutes descriptor values were taken from the published literatures [37–41]. To get a better concept of the intermolecular interaction offered by the new C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase, the system coefficients for C18/GQDs/SiO2 were juxtaposed with those for commercial C18 column ( Table 2). The suitability of LSER model for chemical interpretation of C18/GQDs/SiO2 was justified by experimental log k versus calculated log k plot ( Fig. 12) in the tested mobile phase composition. None of the solutes appeared to be outliers and the adjusted coefficient of determination (R2 adj) was 0.91, which indicated the data calculated from the LSER model were reliable.

The system constants (e, s, a, b, v, d - and d +), obtained through a multilinear regression of the retention data for a certain number of solutes with known descriptors, reflect the magnitude of difference for that particular property between the mobile phase and stationary phase. Thus, if a particular coefficient is numerically large, then any solute having the complementary property will interact very strongly with either the mobile phase (if the coefficient is negative) or the stationary phase (if the coefficient is positive). From Table 2, we could observe that C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase displayed typical RP features like the C18 stationary phase, as reflected by the large and opposite b and v coefficients but with peculiar character in the aspect of e and d - parameter. The dominant and opposite parts taken by the b and v coefficients are characteristic of partitioning systems where water is one of the two phases [37].

The v parameter was an indication of the chromatographic system's tendency to interact with solute through hydrophobic interaction. A positive v value meant a stronger interaction between solute and stationary phase than mobile phase. Compare the v values of the two stationary phases, we see C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase was hydrophobic due to the long aliphatic chain, but less hydrophobic than pure C18 stationary phase, resulting from the multiple hydrophilic moieties of GQDs.

The e parameter reflected the system's tendency to interact with solute through π-π interaction. C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase had a larger and positive e value compared with C18 stationary phase, signifying its high capability of π-π stacking. This behaviour can be attributed to the large π-electron system of GQDs.

The s coefficient gives the tendency of stationary phase to interact with dipolar and/or polarizable solutes. In the present case, they were negative, indicating that dipole-dipole interactions were stronger between solute and mobile phase than between solute and stationary phase. This was reasonable since ACN and water were much more polar than the stationary phases.

The a coefficient denotes the hydrogen-bond basicity or hydrogen-bond acceptor properties of the stationary phase, because acidic solutes (having a positive A coefficient) will interact with a basic phase. Interestingly, C18/GQDs/SiO2 exhibited very small negative a value (-0.004) which was much larger than that of C18 stationary phase. This would probably indicate the hydrogen-bonding acceptance ability of GQDs. These values were confirmed the experimental observations since C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase showed higher selectivity for phenols which could be seen as hydrogen-bonding donors.

The b coefficient is a measure of the hydrogen-bond acidity of the stationary phase, because basic solutes (having large B values) will interact with an acidic phase. C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase had large and negative b value similar to C18 stationary phase. Such large and opposite b and v coefficients are always observed in RP-LC systems.

The d coefficient is a measure of the electrostatic interaction of the stationary phase. Both d - and d + were negative for C18 stationary phase, which indicated that the charged solute had stronger interaction with mobile phase than stationary phase. But for C18/GQDs/SiO2 phase, it had a positive d - and a larger negative d + than C18 stationary phase, which indicated the presence of electrostatic attraction interaction with negatively charged solute and electrostatic repulsion interaction with positively charged solute. The electrostatic attraction property of C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase might be due to the quaternary amine groups since nitrogen atoms were doped in GQDs.

In conclusion, C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase could provide typical RP retention mechanism under RP-LC conditions with distinctive characteristics, such as its behaviour in strengthened π-π interaction and enhanced hydrogen-bonding acceptance ability. The conclusion drawn from the mathematic model agreed with the experimental results that π-π and hydrogen-bonding interactions made contributions to the separation.

The stability is one of the essential parameters of the developed C18/GQDs/SiO2 column. The retention of six model analytes was studied over 80 h of continuous operation with 30% ACN/H2O as eluent. The retention times (t R) and plate counts (N) for all model analytes were essentially constant, see Fig. S7. A single column has been used off and on in our laboratory for 6 months with no significant loss of column performance.

The run-to-run and day-to-day repeatability and column-to-column reproducibility of C18/GQDs/SiO2 column were tested by the separation of phenols. The relative standard deviation (RSD) values of k, peak area (A) and N were shown in Table S2. The RSD values of k were less than 0.56% (n = 10, run-to-run), 0.62% (n = 3, day-to-day) and 5.11% (n = 3, column-to-column), showing satisfactory repeatability and reproducibility. The run-to-run overlapped chromatograms were shown in Fig. S8.

Considering that silica is stable in the pH range of 2–8, we examined the stability of this column in pH 2.5 and 7.6, respectively. 60:40 MeOH/20 mM ammonium formate (pH 2.5 and 7.6) was passed through the column for fixed periods and chromatography separation was carried out under the same condition and the process repeated (Figs. S9 and S10). Both acid and base exposure experiments were carried out until 25 h passed. Test results showed this column was stable in the pH range of 2.5–7.6.

The stability of this column under high percentage of water and organic solvent was tested respectively. Eluent containing 95% H2O or ACN was passed through the column continuously and chromatography was carried out after fixed periods (Figs. S11 and S12). In all cases the retention time were stable.

The organic solvent tolerance of this column was tested by eluting it with tetrahydrofuran and acetone. After eluting it with tetrahydrofuran and acetone for fixed periods, the column performance was tested by separation of anilines (Fig. S13 and S14). Test results showed this column had good tolerance with tetrahydrofuran and acetone.
It was found that this C18/GQDs/SiO2 column was stable in the pH range of 2.5–7.6, 5–100% organic solvent could be used as eluent, tetrahydrofuran and acetone could be used as organic additives.

The column efficiencies of all the tested analytes were calculated. Phenols, anilines and aromatic acids got an average column efficiency of 21,492, 19,977 and 20,591 plates/m, respectively. Nucleosides and bases had an average column efficiency of 25922 plates/m. PAHs and alkylbenzenes got an average column efficiency of 14,743 and 15,486 plates/m, respectively. In general, this C18/GQDs/SiO2 column showed satisfactory chromatographic performance for several kinds of compounds.

A novel C18/GQDs/SiO2 stationary phase was prepared and evaluated by several kinds of compounds including anilines, phenols, PAHs, alkylbenzenes, aromatic acids, alkaloids, nucleosides and bases in RP-LC and HILIC. This novel stationary phase showed satisfactory separation performance for all the tested analytes. Better separation performance for certain compounds over commercial C18 column was achieved, which was because the presence of GQDs in the stationary phase introduced some interactions such as π-π and hydrogen-bonding interactions. Through investigating the impact of mobile phase composition on retention, it was found this new stationary phase showed typical characteristics of hydrophobic/hydrophilic chromatography. To get an in-depth understanding of the retention mechanism, LSER model was established for both C18/GQDs/SiO2 and C18 columns. From data analysis, it was concluded that C18/GQDs/SiO2 could provide strengthened π-π interaction and hydrogen-bonding acceptance ability except for hydrophobic interaction. We may find that GQDs or other CNMs with multiple properties including hydrophobic, hydrophilic, π-π stacking and hydrogen-bonding characteristics are good functional materials to improve the separation performance of the conventional C18 stationary phase in HPLC.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405162, 21405161, 21675163 and 21702210).
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.10.005
